differentials.raw: bile peritonitis

differentials.raw: biliary fistula: external (to skin / hollow viscus), internal (to intestine / bronchus), biliovascular (to hepatic artery, portal / hepatic vein) Rx:     conservative treatment in up to 80% in adults + 97% in children; transcatheter embolization

differentials.raw: crush / compression injury: direct force; near spine

differentials.raw: burst injury: sudden increase in intraluminal pressure

differentials.name: shear injury: rapid deceleration at points of transition between mobile and fixed bowel portions Location:  jejunum distal to ligament of Treitz > duodenum > ascending colon at ileocecal valve > descending colon > distal ileum near ileocecal valve
differentials.raw: shear injury: rapid deceleration at points of transition between mobile and fixed bowel portions Location:  jejunum distal to ligament of Treitz > duodenum > ascending colon at ileocecal valve > descending colon > distal ileum near ileocecal valve

differentials.raw: Classic triad (in only 33%):

differentials.name: abdominal pain + tenderness
differentials.raw: abdominal pain + tenderness (100% sensitive)

differentials.name: abdominal rigidity; absent / decreased bowel sounds
differentials.raw: abdominal rigidity; absent / decreased bowel sounds

differentials.name: ↑ temperature + heart rate; ↓ urine output over 24 hours
differentials.raw: ↑ temperature + heart rate; ↓ urine output over 24 hours

differentials.raw: lap belt ecchymosis (not highly correlated)

differentials.name: Lavage:  90% sensitive for hemoperitoneum → compromises interpretation of CT exam N.B.:     clinical signs + symptoms may be delayed for 24 hours
differentials.raw: Lavage:  90% sensitive for hemoperitoneum → compromises interpretation of CT exam N.B.:     clinical signs + symptoms may be delayed for 24 hours (increasing mortality to 65%)

differentials.raw: IV  severe crush injury

differentials.raw: leukocytosis, ↑ serum amylase activity

differentials.raw: transection (fracture) = hypoattenuating linear findings with separated parenchyma

context: Prevalence:   2nd most frequently injured abdominal viscus Associated with:   splenic injury in 45% Location:  right (posterior segment) > left lobe Site:     perivascular, paralleling right + middle hepatic arteries + posterior branches of right portal vein, avulsion of right hepatic vein from IVC (13%) ◊  Left lobe injuries are more often associated with damage to duodenum, pancreas, transverse colon CECT: √  liver laceration = predominantly irregular linear branching / round regions of low attenuation: (a) superficial ≤ 3 cm deep; (b) > 3 cm in depth ◊  Most frequently identified injury pat
